params.text:
Raising and reporting a concern

If you suspect abuse or neglect, act promptly. First make sure the person is safe and, if someone is in immediate danger or a crime is happening, call 999. If someone needs urgent medical help, get it. Then follow the safeguarding steps below.

  • Preserve evidence - do not clean up, move or tidy anything that might be needed later, and avoid asking leading questions.
  • Record the facts - write down what you saw or were told as soon as possible, using the person's own words where you can, with the date, time and your name. Keep opinion separate from fact.
  • Report without delay - tell your manager or your organisation's safeguarding lead, following your local safeguarding policy and its timescales.

Your provider must then raise the concern with the local authority, which is the lead agency for safeguarding enquiries under the Care Act. If the concern is about your own manager, or you feel it is not being acted on, you can raise it directly with the local authority yourself.

Keeping the person at the centre

When someone discloses abuse to you, stay calm, listen, take them seriously and reassure them that they were right to tell you. Do not promise to keep it secret - explain honestly that you have a duty to pass the information on to keep them safe. Tell them what will happen next and, wherever possible, involve them in decisions about the response.

params.question: A person you support quietly tells you a colleague has been rough with them but begs you not to tell anyone. What should you do?

answers.text: Reassure them, explain you have a duty to report it to keep them safe, and pass the concern to your safeguarding lead.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. You must never promise secrecy. Be honest that you have to report, record the disclosure, and raise it promptly.

answers.text: Promise to keep it secret so they keep trusting you.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. You cannot promise confidentiality when someone may be at risk. It would leave the person unprotected.

answers.text: Handle it quietly by having a word with the colleague yourself.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. It is not your role to investigate or confront. This could increase risk and disrupt a proper enquiry.

answers.text: Ignore it, as it is one person's word against another.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Every disclosure must be taken seriously and reported. It is not for you to weigh up whose account is true.
